ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 9, 5, 10, 13, 88693, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_missing_relations_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_missing_relations_v2_src"."account" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.account  (80 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `tier` text NULL
- `region` text NULL
- `industry` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.asset  (320 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `serial` text NULL
- `warranty` text NULL
- `client` text NULL
- `project` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.contact  (152 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `full_name` text NULL
- `email` text NULL
- `account_ref` text NULL
- `company_name` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.opportunity  (221 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `stage` text NULL
- `amount` double precision NULL
- `customer_number` text NULL
- `account_name` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.project  (112 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `status` text NULL
- `go_live` text NULL
- `client_id` text NULL
- `opportunity_ref` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

# Target schema


## Account
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `ERP_Number__c` text
- `Customer_Tier__c` text in (Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum)
- `Region__c` text
- `Industry` text
- `Website` text
- `BillingCity` text
- `BillingCountry` text
- `Legacy_Customer_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Contact
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `FirstName` text
- `LastName` text NOT NULL
- `Email` text
- `Phone` text
- `Title` text
- `Role__c` text in (Decision Maker, End User, Technical Contact, Executive Sponsor)
- `Preferred_Language__c` text in (DE, EN, FR, ES, IT)
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Contact_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Opportunity
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `StageName` text NOT NULL in (Prospecting, Qualification, Needs Analysis, Value Proposition, Id. Decision Makers, Perception Analysis, Proposal/Price Quote, Negotiation/Review, Closed Won, Closed Lost)
- `CloseDate` text NOT NULL
- `Amount` double precision
- `CurrencyIsoCode` text
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Project__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Project_Status__c` text in (Active, Completed, In Planning, On Hold, Cancelled)
- `Go_Live_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Opportunity__c` text
- `Legacy_Project_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.


Produce the dbt project that transforms the source into the target. Write one model file per target table listed above — all tables are required.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 9, 5, 10, 13, 88701, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 9, 5, 10, 13, 88927, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f4548-975f-772e-ba59-d4eb9a748615', conversation_id='019f4548-975f-772e-ba59-d4ea8817dafd')

ModelResponse(parts=[ThinkingPart(content='Good, I now have a good picture of the data. Let me analyze the key patterns:

**Source to Target mappings:**

1. **Account**: `account` → `Account`
   - `id` (ACC-XXXX) → `Id`, `Legacy_Customer_ID__c`
   - `name` → `Name`
   - `tier` → `Customer_Tier__c` (already valid: Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum)
   - `region` → `Region__c`
   - `industry` → `Industry`
   - Other fields like Website, BillingCity, etc. are NOT in source → NULL or defaults

2. **Contact**: `contact` → `Contact`
   - `id` (CON-XXXXX) → `Id`, `Legacy_Contact_ID__c`
   - `full_name` → split into `FirstName`, `LastName`
   - `email` → `Email`
   - `account_ref` (ACC-XXXX) → `AccountId` (must match Account.Id format)
   - Other fields like Phone, Title, Role__c, Preferred_Language__c NOT in source → NULL

3. **Opportunity**: `opportunity` → `Opportunity`
   - `id` (OPP-XXXXX) → `Id`, `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c`
   - `name` → `Name`
   - `stage` → `StageName` (needs mapping to target enum)
   - `amount` → `Amount`
   - `customer_number` (KD-XXXX) → need to map to Account.Id via account_name or customer_number
   - The AccountId needs to come from matching customer_number "KD-1001" → Account id "ACC-1001"
   
4. **Project__c**: `project` → `Project__c`
   - `id` (PROJ-XXXXX) → `Id`, `Legacy_Project_ID__c`
   - `name` → `Name`
   - `status` → `Project_Status__c` (needs mapping to target enum)
   - `go_live` → `Go_Live_Date__c` (already YYYY-MM-DD format)
   - `client_id` (ACC-XXXX) → `Account__c` (must match Account.Id)
   - `opportunity_ref` (OPP-XXXXX) → `Opportunity__c` (matches Opportunity.Id)

5. **Installed_Asset__c**: `asset` → `Installed_Asset__c`
   - `id` (AST-XXXXX) → `Id`, `Legacy_Asset_ID__c`
   - `name` → `Name`
   - `serial` → `Serial_Number__c`
   - `warranty` → `Warranty_End_Date__c` (already YYYY-MM-DD format)
   - `client` → `Account__c` — but here the client field can be either a code like "ACC-XXXX" or a company name like "Gnatz AG", which means I'll need to resolve account references by checking if it matches an Account.Id pattern or falls back to matching against Account.Name
   - `project` → `Project__c`

The main challenge is that the asset table stores client references inconsistently — sometimes as account codes, sometimes as account names. For the Opportunity mapping, I'll need to derive the Account relationship by parsing the customer_number format (KD-XXXX) and joining it back through either the Account.Name or directly matching it with the opportunity's account_name field. ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 9, 5, 10, 13, 88693, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_missing_relations_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_missing_relations_v2_src"."account" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.account  (80 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `tier` text NULL
- `region` text NULL
- `industry` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.asset  (320 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `serial` text NULL
- `warranty` text NULL
- `client` text NULL
- `project` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.contact  (152 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `full_name` text NULL
- `email` text NULL
- `account_ref` text NULL
- `company_name` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.opportunity  (221 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `stage` text NULL
- `amount` double precision NULL
- `customer_number` text NULL
- `account_name` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.project  (112 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `status` text NULL
- `go_live` text NULL
- `client_id` text NULL
- `opportunity_ref` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

# Target schema


## Account
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `ERP_Number__c` text
- `Customer_Tier__c` text in (Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum)
- `Region__c` text
- `Industry` text
- `Website` text
- `BillingCity` text
- `BillingCountry` text
- `Legacy_Customer_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Contact
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `FirstName` text
- `LastName` text NOT NULL
- `Email` text
- `Phone` text
- `Title` text
- `Role__c` text in (Decision Maker, End User, Technical Contact, Executive Sponsor)
- `Preferred_Language__c` text in (DE, EN, FR, ES, IT)
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Contact_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Opportunity
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `StageName` text NOT NULL in (Prospecting, Qualification, Needs Analysis, Value Proposition, Id. Decision Makers, Perception Analysis, Proposal/Price Quote, Negotiation/Review, Closed Won, Closed Lost)
- `CloseDate` text NOT NULL
- `Amount` double precision
- `CurrencyIsoCode` text
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Project__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Project_Status__c` text in (Active, Completed, In Planning, On Hold, Cancelled)
- `Go_Live_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Opportunity__c` text
- `Legacy_Project_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.


Produce the dbt project that transforms the source into the target. Write one model file per target table listed above — all tables are required.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 9, 5, 10, 13, 88701, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 9, 5, 10, 13, 88927, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f4548-975f-772e-ba59-d4eb9a748615', conversation_id='019f4548-975f-772e-ba59-d4ea8817dafd')

ModelResponse(parts=[ThinkingPart(content='Let me analyze this task carefully. I need to:

ModelResponse(parts=[ThinkingPart(content='Good, I now have a good picture of the data. Let me analyze the key patterns:

   - `id` (ACC-XXXX) → `Id`, `Legacy_Customer_ID__c`

   - `tier` → `Customer_Tier__c` (already valid: Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum)
   - `region` → `Region__c`
   - `industry` → `Industry`
   - Other fields like Website, BillingCity, etc. are NOT in source → NULL or defaults

2. **Contact**: `contact` → `Contact`
   - `id` (CON-XXXXX) → `Id`, `Legacy_Contact_ID__c`
   - `full_name` → split into `FirstName`, `LastName`
   - `email` → `Email`
   - `account_ref` (ACC-XXXX) → `AccountId` (must match Account.Id format)
   - Other fields like Phone, Title, Role__c, Preferred_Language__c NOT in source → NULL

3. **Opportunity**: `opportunity` → `Opportunity`
   - `id` (OPP-XXXXX) → `Id`, `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c`
   - `name` → `Name`
   - `stage` → `StageName` (needs mapping to target enum)
   - `amount` → `Amount`
   - `customer_number` (KD-XXXX) → need to map to Account.Id via account_name or customer_number
   - The AccountId needs to come from matching customer_number "KD-1001" → Account id "ACC-1001"
   
4. **Project__c**: `project` → `Project__c`
   - `id` (PROJ-XXXXX) → `Id`, `Legacy_Project_ID__c`
   - `name` → `Name`
   - `status` → `Project_Status__c` (needs mapping to target enum)
   - `go_live` → `Go_Live_Date__c` (already YYYY-MM-DD format)
   - `client_id` (ACC-XXXX) → `Account__c` (must match Account.Id)
   - `opportunity_ref` (OPP-XXXXX) → `Opportunity__c` (matches Opportunity.Id)

5. **Installed_Asset__c**: `asset` → `Installed_Asset__c`
   - `id` (AST-XXXXX) → `Id`, `Legacy_Asset_ID__c`
   - `name` → `Name`
   - `serial` → `Serial_Number__c`
   - `warranty` → `Warranty_End_Date__c` (already YYYY-MM-DD format)
   - `client` → `Account__c` — but here the client field can be either a code like "ACC-XXXX" or a company name like "Gnatz AG", which means I'll need to resolve account references by checking if it matches an Account.Id pattern or falls back to matching against Account.Name
   - `project` → `Project__c`

The main challenge is that the asset table stores client references inconsistently — sometimes as account codes, sometimes as account names. ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 9, 5, 10, 13, 88693, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_missing_relations_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_missing_relations_v2_src"."account" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.account  (80 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `tier` text NULL
- `region` text NULL
- `industry` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.asset  (320 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `serial` text NULL
- `warranty` text NULL
- `client` text NULL
- `project` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 9, 5, 10, 13, 88693, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_missing_relations_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_missing_relations_v2_src"."account" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.account  (80 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `tier` text NULL
- `region` text NULL
- `industry` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.asset  (320 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `serial` text NULL
- `warranty` text NULL
- `client` text NULL
- `project` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.contact  (152 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `full_name` text NULL
- `email` text NULL
- `account_ref` text NULL
- `company_name` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.opportunity  (221 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `stage` text NULL
- `amount` double precision NULL
- `customer_number` text NULL
- `account_name` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.project  (112 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `status` text NULL
- `go_live` text NULL
- `client_id` text NULL
- `opportunity_ref` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

# Target schema


## Account
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `ERP_Number__c` text
- `Customer_Tier__c` text in (Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum)
- `Region__c` text
- `Industry` text
- `Website` text
- `BillingCity` text
- `BillingCountry` text
- `Legacy_Customer_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Contact
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `FirstName` text
- `LastName` text NOT NULL
- `Email` text
- `Phone` text
- `Title` text
- `Role__c` text in (Decision Maker, End User, Technical Contact, Executive Sponsor)
- `Preferred_Language__c` text in (DE, EN, FR, ES, IT)
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Contact_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Opportunity
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `StageName` text NOT NULL in (Prospecting, Qualification, Needs Analysis, Value Proposition, Id. Decision Makers, Perception Analysis, Proposal/Price Quote, Negotiation/Review, Closed Won, Closed Lost)
- `CloseDate` text NOT NULL
- `Amount` double precision
- `CurrencyIsoCode` text
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Project__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Project_Status__c` text in (Active, Completed, In Planning, On Hold, Cancelled)
- `Go_Live_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Opportunity__c` text
- `Legacy_Project_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.


Produce the dbt project that transforms the source into the target. Write one model file per target table listed above — all tables are required.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 9, 5, 10, 13, 88701, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 9, 5, 10, 13, 88927, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f4548-975f-772e-ba59-d4eb9a748615', conversation_id='019f4548-975f-772e-ba59-d4ea8817dafd')

   - `id` (CON-XXXXX) → `Id`, `Legacy_Contact_ID__c`
   - `full_name` → split into `FirstName`, `LastName`
   - `email` → `Email`
   - `account_ref` (ACC-XXXX) → `AccountId` (must match Account.Id format)
   - Other fields like Phone, Title, Role__c, Preferred_Language__c NOT in source → NULL

3. **Opportunity**: `opportunity` → `Opportunity`
   - `id` (OPP-XXXXX) → `Id`, `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c`
   - `name` → `Name`
   - `stage` → `StageName` (needs mapping to target enum)
   - `amount` → `Amount`
   - `customer_number` (KD-XXXX) → need to map to Account.Id via account_name or customer_number
   - The AccountId needs to come from matching customer_number "KD-1001" → Account id "ACC-1001"
   
4. **Project__c**: `project` → `Project__c`
   - `id` (PROJ-XXXXX) → `Id`, `Legacy_Project_ID__c`
   - `name` → `Name`
   - `status` → `Project_Status__c` (needs mapping to target enum)
   - `go_live` → `Go_Live_Date__c` (already YYYY-MM-DD format)
   - `client_id` (ACC-XXXX) → `Account__c` (must match Account.Id)
   - `opportunity_ref` (OPP-XXXXX) → `Opportunity__c` (matches Opportunity.Id)

5. **Installed_Asset__c**: `asset` → `Installed_Asset__c`
   - `id` (AST-XXXXX) → `Id`, `Legacy_Asset_ID__c`
   - `name` → `Name`
   - `serial` → `Serial_Number__c`

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 9, 5, 10, 13, 88693, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_missing_relations_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.

- `Go_Live_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Opportunity__c` text
- `Legacy_Project_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.


Produce the dbt project that transforms the source into the target. - "Frauke Jacobi Jäckel" → FirstName="Frauke Jacobi", LastName="Jäckel"
- "Gretchen Graf" → FirstName="Gretchen", LastName="Graf"
- Single word "X" → FirstName="", LastName="X"

This seems better. Let me implement:
```sql
CASE WHEN POSITION(' ' IN full_name) > 0 
     THEN TRIM(REGEXP_REPLACE(full_name, '\s\S+$', ''))
     ELSE NULL END AS "FirstName",
CASE WHEN POSITION(' ' IN full_name) > 0 
     THEN SPLIT_PART(full_name, ' ', -1)
     ELSE NULL END AS "LastName"
```

For single word names: FirstName=NULL, LastName=the word. That seems reasonable. The data doesn't seem to have single-word contact names anyway.

Now let me think about one more thing — should I look up any more data? I think I have enough. Let me write all 5 models and sources.yml.

One last consideration: for the `client` field in assets, some values are company names that don't start with 'ACC-'. For these, I need to do a lookup against Account.name. But Account.name can also have variations (typos, spacing issues). I'll do exact matching first, then potentially try a normalized comparison if needed.

Actually, looking at the earlier query results for asset client values, I see names like "Rose Harloff AG", "Weimer GmbH", etc. These appear to match exactly with Account.name values from my earlier query. Let me verify by checking if these are exact matches.

Let me write the models now. Here's my final plan:

### Account
Simple passthrough with NULL defaults for missing fields.

### Contact  
Split full_name into FirstName/LastName. Derive AccountId from account_ref or look up by company_name. All other extra fields = NULL.

### Opportunity
Map stage directly. Derive AccountId from customer_number (KD-XXXX → ACC-XXXX). CloseDate = NULL (no source data).

### Project__c
Direct mapping, status already valid enum format. client_id → Account__c, opportunity_ref → Opportunity__c.

### Installed_Asset__c
Resolve Account__c from client (direct ACC- prefix or look up by name). Project__c from project field.

Now I also need to be mindful of the "fixture_missing_relations_v2" naming — this suggests that some relations are intentionally broken/missing. So my models should handle null foreign keys gracefully rather than failing with cross joins.

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 9, 5, 10, 13, 88693, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_missing_relations_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_missing_relations_v2_src"."account" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.account  (80 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `tier` text NULL
- `region` text NULL
- `industry` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.asset  (320 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `serial` text NULL
- `warranty` text NULL
- `client` text NULL
- `project` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.contact  (152 rows)

Columns:

- `full_name` text NULL
- `email` text NULL

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.opportunity  (221 rows)

Columns:

- `name` text NULL
- `stage` text NULL
- `amount` double precision NULL
- `customer_number` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.project  (112 rows)

Columns:

- `name` text NULL
- `status` text NULL
- `go_live` text NULL
- `client_id` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

# Target schema


## Account
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `ERP_Number__c` text

## Contact
Columns:

- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text

## Opportunity
Columns:

- `CloseDate` text NOT NULL
- `Amount` double precision
- `CurrencyIsoCode` text
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Project__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Project_Status__c` text in (Active, Completed, In Planning, On Hold, Cancelled)
- `Go_Live_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Opportunity__c` text
- `Legacy_Project_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.


Produce the dbt project that transforms the source into the target. Write one model file per target table listed above — all tables are required.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 9, 5, 10, 13, 88701, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 9, 5, 10, 13, 88927, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f4548-975f-772e-ba59-d4eb9a748615', conversation_id='019f4548-975f-772e-ba59-d4ea8817dafd')

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 9, 5, 10, 13, 88693, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_missing_relations_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_missing_relations_v2_src"."account" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.account  (80 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `tier` text NULL
- `region` text NULL
- `industry` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.asset  (320 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `serial` text NULL
- `warranty` text NULL
- `client` text NULL
- `project` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.contact  (152 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `full_name` text NULL
- `email` text NULL
- `account_ref` text NULL
- `company_name` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.opportunity  (221 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `stage` text NULL
- `amount` double precision NULL
- `customer_number` text NULL
- `account_name` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.project  (112 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `status` text NULL
- `go_live` text NULL
- `client_id` text NULL
- `opportunity_ref` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

# Target schema


## Account
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `ERP_Number__c` text
- `Customer_Tier__c` text in (Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum)
- `Region__c` text
- `Industry` text
- `Website` text
- `BillingCity` text
- `BillingCountry` text
- `Legacy_Customer_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Contact
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `FirstName` text
- `LastName` text NOT NULL
- `Email` text
- `Phone` text
- `Title` text
- `Role__c` text in (Decision Maker, End User, Technical Contact, Executive Sponsor)
- `Preferred_Language__c` text in (DE, EN, FR, ES, IT)
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Contact_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Opportunity
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `StageName` text NOT NULL in (Prospecting, Qualification, Needs Analysis, Value Proposition, Id. Decision Makers, Perception Analysis, Proposal/Price Quote, Negotiation/Review, Closed Won, Closed Lost)
- `CloseDate` text NOT NULL
- `Amount` double precision
- `CurrencyIsoCode` text
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Project__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Project_Status__c` text in (Active, Completed, In Planning, On Hold, Cancelled)
- `Go_Live_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Opportunity__c` text
- `Legacy_Project_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.


Produce the dbt project that transforms the source into the target. Write one model file per target table listed above — all tables are required.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 9, 5, 10, 13, 88701, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 9, 5, 10, 13, 88927, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f4548-975f-772e-ba59-d4eb9a748615', conversation_id='019f4548-975f-772e-ba59-d4ea8817dafd')

ModelResponse(parts=[ThinkingPart(content='Good, I now have a good picture of the data. Let me analyze the key patterns:

**Source to Target mappings:**

1. **Account**: `account` → `Account`
   - `id` (ACC-XXXX) → `Id`, `Legacy_Customer_ID__c`
   - `name` → `Name`
   - `tier` → `Customer_Tier__c` (already valid: Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum)
   - `region` → `Region__c`
   - `industry` → `Industry`
   - Other fields like Website, BillingCity, etc. are NOT in source → NULL or defaults

2. **Contact**: `contact` → `Contact`
   - `id` (CON-XXXXX) → `Id`, `Legacy_Contact_ID__c`
   - `full_name` → split into `FirstName`, `LastName`
   - `email` → `Email`
   - `account_ref` (ACC-XXXX) → `AccountId` (must match Account.Id format)
   - Other fields like Phone, Title, Role__c, Preferred_Language__c NOT in source → NULL

3. **Opportunity**: `opportunity` → `Opportunity`

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
